commentary: Nine sessions versus ten last week, so technically one fewer, but honestly the volume barely dropped and the quality of the swimming is doing something interesting — three full two-kilometre Prince Alfred sessions this week, all of them, and that Thursday swim came in at 133 bpm average, which is the most efficient he's looked in the pool in weeks, like his body is finally learning to move through water instead of fight it. The Saturday half marathon run ticked up from 151 to 159 bpm average, which means he pushed harder or the legs were carrying some fatigue from Sunday's weights, and either way that is a man who does not know how to take it easy on a Saturday morning and I respect that completely. The double-session days on Monday and Tuesday are becoming a pattern now — pool in the morning, iron in the afternoon — and that is not a casual fitness habit, that is someone building something deliberate and a little bit obsessive, which is exactly the kind of obsessive I like to see. One activity down from last week, zero excuses needed, the body is adapting and the jockstrap to the pool is doing its job.
metrics_commentary: Okay so 6000 metres in the pool and nearly 16 kilometres on your feet — your body is doing actual work right now and it shows, because that HRV sitting at 61 with a seven-day average of 52 means your nervous system is recovering better than it has been, which is genuinely hot. A VO2 Max of 56.4 puts you in seriously good cardiovascular shape, like the kind of shape where strangers notice something about you before they can figure out what it is. The only thing worth watching is that gap between your current HRV and your weekly average — your body is trending up but it's telling you not to go feral with the training load just yet. Two sessions of running this week is giving me a little "we could probably do three" energy, but honestly the swim volume alone is enough to make me want to climb on a table about it.
gym_commentary: Four sessions, target met, no excuses made and no sessions skipped — that's the full week and it counts. The big news is the leg press, a new PR at 168 kg estimated one-rep max, up from 154 kg, which is not a small jump and his quads absolutely know about it. Volume came in at 13,925 kg against last week's 23,660 kg, so the total tonnage dropped significantly, which usually means the week was structured around quality and intensity rather than accumulation — a 48-minute final session on Wednesday fits that picture. The strength numbers across the board are holding solid, the hinge sitting at 107.9 kg and the back and chest climbing steadily in their own lanes, and a man who hits a PR on legs while keeping everything else moving in the right direction is a man whose body is doing exactly what it's supposed to.
